candidates.stmt:
Representing people, not a party

“Do you feel like I do? That political  parties are not listening to the concerns of local people? That they have lost touch with the wishes of Bishopstoke? 

With a background in local government and a masters degree I felt it was time to give back to my community. These concerns inspired me to stand up for Bishopstoke & Fair Oak. I've been privileged and proud to have received so much support from my local community, now currently representing local people on Hampshire County Council (since 2022) Eastleigh Borough Council (Since 2018) and Bishopstoke Parish (since 2016). successfully run for the Parish Council election in 2016. Together with the local community and my Independent colleagues I have helped to convince the Local Planning Inspector that the potentially 5000+ buildings proposal in the north of our villages would be destructive and I am now actively seeking the same area be protected as a nature reserve. After three years of campaigning for safer indication of the junior school and slower speeds, I was thrilled, that within my first 6months at Hampshire, warning lights and a 20mph limit has been installed. I  have sought to influence decision makers. 

Including: speaking up on behalf of residents at local meetings; helping residents experiencing difficulties with developments; challenging policies on fly-tipping; and, pushed for new footpaths. Bishopstoke has always been home. I  went to the local Infant and Junior school,  I’m a member of Bishopstoke Players  and Bishopstoke History Society. I grew up with its ancient woodland & Itchen and want to see these preserved for future generations”

If elected I will: ✓ continue to fight eco-vandalism that could devastate our ancient woodland, countryside and the Itchen  ✓ keep local people informed of the decisions that will affect us and listen to their views ✓ support services and policies which contribute to the health and wellbeing of local people, our environment and wi

After nine years of military service, I ran a successful business for over 30 years while continuing to serve the community as a special constable, retained firefighter, and community first responder.

As a parish councillor in neighbouring Allbrook, I remain committed to practical action and strong local representation.

My wife and family grew up locally, and through years of volunteering I have built lasting connections across the area. I understand the challenges residents face — protecting vital services, improving infrastructure, and keeping our community safe, welcoming, and thriving.

I believe in listening first, working collaboratively, and delivering real results that benefit everyone. I am community-focused and dedicated to putting residents before politics.

Anne is Bishopstoke’s longest serving councillor, and the only councillor to have served on Bishopstoke Parish Council since it was created in 1996. A former Deputy Leader of the Borough Council and Cabinet Member for Social Policy, Anne has campaigned for traffic relief on Bishopstoke Road and secured land at Stoke Common Park for public access after its earlier allocation for housing. Anne has been Chair of Bishopstoke Parish Council and also Chair of the County Parish Council Association. She is also involved in several charities and community organisations providing advice, assistance and community facilities where needed.

I am proud to stand for Bishopstoke, where I have lived for over 13 years and where my children attend local schools. I work in a local college, so I understand the importance of education, opportunity and strong community support. I care deeply about the area and the people who live here. I want to protect our environment, support local services, and ensure residents have a real voice in decisions that affect them. I will work hard, listen to concerns, and stand up for Bishopstoke every day and always put our community first in everything I do for local residents.

Rupert was elected to the Borough Council in 2002 and is Botley’s longest-serving councillor. As Cabinet Member at Eastleigh for the environment, Rupert has championed recycling, air and water quality, and drainage concerns.  A past Mayor, he is also a Parish and County Councillor. Rupert led the campaign for Botley’s by-pass, opposed for many years by the County Council, and has recently won his campaign to get pavements along more of Kings Copse Avenue. Next to get the speed limit down! He is working to secure more doctors’ appointments and facilities at Botley Surgery, and a pharmacy at Boorley.

I’m standing as a Green candidate because I care deeply about Botley and Hampshire, where I was raised and have lived locally for seven years. I understand the issues residents face and believe politics should prioritise listening, transparency, and long-term wellbeing over short-term profit. My priorities include protecting green spaces, supporting strong communities, and ensuring development benefits residents without straining infrastructure. I support sustainable planning, improved public transport, safer walking and cycling routes to reduce congestion and pollution. I am committed to addressing the cost-of-living crisis through affordable housing and community initiatives. I will represent residents with integrity and dedication.

Tonia is Bursledon’s longest-serving Borough Councillor and is Cabinet Member for Health at the Borough Council, leading on working with the NHS to improve our local health services and on community safety. She is also a member of the County Council where one of her roles is holding the Police & Crime Commissioner to account. Last year she was asked by Hound Parish Council to take on chairing the Parish Council that covers Butlocks Heath in her Borough ward. Tonia is pressing the County Council to tackle potholes and the dreadful condition of Hamble Lane and other local roads.

Keith has lived in Eastleigh for over 40 years and has been active in local politics most of that time.  Inspired to join the Green Party by Zack Polanski's national leadership, Keith is keen to implement Green policies locally. He would work to improve water quality in our rivers, reduce pollution, ensure the environment and biodiversity are protected and enhanced in planning decisions, improve public transport and support sustainable local business.

Vote for Frankie Coultas on Thursday 7th May standing for TUSC for a better Bursledon and Hound North
Why vote TUSC?
•	No cuts to council jobs and services
•	No increase in council tax, rents and charges
•	£15 an hour minimum wage
•	Affordable and efficient public transport including working with trade unions to establish a better bus service in Bursledon and Hound to reduce air pollution and connect communities across Eastleigh
•	Tackle anti-social behavior including dangerous drivers on Hamble Lane, Lowford Hill and Portsmouth Road
•	Environmental initiatives including holding to account utility companies who pollute our waterways such as the Hamble River and working with other councilors in other wards fighting against the destructive gravel pit proposal in Hamble Airfield
Elect a fighting, anti-cuts councilor who will  campaign to end austerity, reverse cuts and protect our services, safety and the environment as well as delivering jobs, homes and fully-funded services for all.

Alan is Chandler’s Ford’s longest serving councillor. First elected to Eastleigh Borough Council in 1993 he has served on the Council’s Cabinet with responsibility for leisure, has chaired the Council’s Local Area Committee and also represented the area on Hampshire County Council for eight years. Alan has been a member of Chandler’s Ford Parish Council since it started in 2010. Living in Chandler’s Ford since 1971, his children went to local schools. Alan wants to continue to serve the people of Chandler’s Ford the area he loves and knows so well, and get the County to fix the potholes!

Dave was born and raised in Chandler’s Ford and is proud to still call it home. He went to Scantabout and Thornden School, the same schools his children attended. His son is still at Thornden now. Dave’s elder parents also live in the ward, so he sees the issues that matter to residents at every stage of life. Protecting local green spaces, improving local services and facilities for young people, and ensuring the community’s voice is heard are important to him. Working towards a greener future and advocating for issues that matter to the community are his priorities.

I am a 7th Generation former soldier. I served in 16th Independent Parachute Brigade, was seconded to the FCO and carried out security duties at Embassies in conflict areas.

I served in Surrey Police after leaving the forces. I am now retired and work as a Physiotherapy Associate Practitioner with the Local NHS community teams. My wife is a Clinical lead locally with the NHS. We have two daughters 1 is a primary school teacher in Yateley and the other works as a projects manager with Southampton Football club.

I have lived in the Forest for about 20 years, my wife was born at the former Fenwick Hospital in Ashurst, her family are lifetime residents here.

Problems with the constituency.

The New Forest is a beautiful place to live for those that can afford to, but take a walk into some of the poorer areas and see the truth. Poor families on council waiting lists, with no houses being built. Planning regulations of a draconian nature exist to throw up barriers instead of facilitating access.

The council refusing to take responsibility instead of doing the “right thing”. Food banks in Totton and Backfields and Hythe because people cannot afford to buy food due to high prices.  Access to services Drs and Dentists, public transport to the General Hospital and other medical services.

The New forest is so poorly served by our local ambulance services that some cannot attend or afford a taxi, which is often the advice given. In the Forest itself, we see younger generations driven away because of high costs in both rent and lack of housing, leading to population displacements we can little afford to accept. We have our community nurses struggling to meet demands as the area is so large they cannot reach everyone and have very few staff to do it. Lymington Hospital is always shouting loud for staff to perform the wonderful services they do.

The council continually failing its people to provide good quality affordable housing and services. Employment has been decreasing

Bors has trained as a social scientist and a climate scientist. His expert area is climate migration.
He runs a small research business within healthcare.

His evening job is climate activism.

He is Chair of the Sustainability Committee at the British Healthcare Business Intelligence Association. 
He regularly gives talks about the climate emergency to audiences across the South.

Bors has lived in Chandler's Ford for over 10 years, is an active member of the community and a scout leader.

Bors has 3 boys, all attending local schools. His vision is to leave a fair and liveable planet to future generations.

Cameron Spencer is the youngest member of the Borough Council Lib Dem team and was first elected to the Council in 2022. Cameron has worked as the MP’s Chief of Staff in Westminster and Eastleigh, providing a vital link between Parliament since 2024. Cameron is also a member of Eastleigh Town Council, representing Brookwood Ward. He has been a trustee for a local charity, and in his spare time, is a keen runner and hockey player. Cameron is campaigning to get the missing Chickenhall Link By-Pass after the County Council abandoned support for it, to reduce town centre traffic.

I have lived in Eastleigh for over 35 years and currently have run my shop in Eastleigh High Street for over 5 years. I’ve seen our community struggle while the Council wastes money and ignores local people. I’m standing to fight for our small businesses and comunity, tackle antisocial behaviour, and protect our green spaces from overdevelopment

I am a local resident deeply rooted in Eastleigh, focused on our community, not personal ambition. Having lived here since I was seven, I am committed to serving those who call Eastleigh home.

My priority is improving quality of life, better healthcare, affordable housing, local jobs, and protecting our environment. Crucially, I believe in society's obligation to protect the vulnerable.

I aim to strengthen the Green Party's voice on the council. With representation, we can join more committees and deliver meaningful change for Eastleigh.

Together, we can build a greener, fairer future where no one is left behind.

I have lived in Eastleigh for the past decade and am proud to call it home. I’m standing in this by-election because I believe our town deserves better. After 30 years of Liberal Democrat control at Eastleigh Borough Council, too many people feel things are going backwards. It’s time for a fresh start.

If elected, I will focus on the basics: fixing our roads, tackling antisocial behaviour, revitalising the town centre, protecting and investing in our green spaces, and fighting cuts to local bus services. I will push for stronger action on street cleaning, better support for independent businesses, and a real effort to bring pride back to our high street.

I also support Labour’s plans to reform local government. Replacing the borough and county council structures with a single unitary authority and a directly elected mayor would mean clearer accountability, better value for money, and stronger leadership for our community.

I’m passionate about making a real difference for Eastleigh; I hope I can count on your support.

Steve is standing for re-election to the Borough Council for North Ward having first been elected in 2022.  Steve has worked for his diverse community of Boyatt Wood, Allbrook and the Twyford Road area of Eastleigh town over his four years. Steve works as a manager of a group of dental practices.  He cycles and has undertaken fundraising including a triathlon across to the Isle of Wight for The Courage Foundation which supports children who have lost a brother or sister to enjoy themselves again through funding of activities such as passes to family theme parks, and also cancer charities.

I have lived in Eastleigh since 2016 and am standing in Eastleigh North. I am heavily invested in the Eastleigh area. My children attend nursery and school in the Borough. Professionally, I run a consultancy near Southampton Airport advising firms on governance and financial regulation.

Eastleigh residents have been feeling the impact of rising council tax for some time. Hampshire’s finances are under severe pressure. Reserves are being depleted, and a significant funding gap is emerging that threatens us all.

My priorities are clear:

(1) challenging wasteful spending and demanding proper scrutiny of council finances;

(2) contesting decisions that increase costs for residents without improving services; and

(3) focusing spending on core services that people in Eastleigh rely on.

At work, I deal with complex financial issues. I would bring that same discipline locally, holding decision-makers to account and standing up for residents facing rising costs and declining value.

Paul has represented South Ward on the Borough Council since 2011 and is a member of Eastleigh Town Council.  Professionally, he is a train driver with over 25 years’ experience. Paul is a past Deputy Leader of the Borough and Cabinet Member for Social Policy and Regeneration. Heavily involved in housing, Paul helped formulate housing policies for the council’s new Eastbrooke homes, was a key member of the team bringing forward temporary homeless accommodation at Bampton House and the redevelopment of the old Post Office to provide the council with new social housing units at Postmark Place in the town centre.

I’m standing with Reform UK for election to Eastleigh Borough Council and as your Prospective Parliamentary Candidate for the General Election this year because I’m proud to call Eastleigh my home, having lived in the borough for 13 years and the town centre for three years.

As a taxpayer, I have become increasingly disappointed and frustrated in the level of public services provided by the Council and its stakeholders.

Once elected, I will work tirelessly to slash the unnecessary bureaucracy and red tape to improve the services we receive from the Council which, in my view, have been deteriorating year on year.

We all deserve value for money public services that deliver tangible results; be that something as basic as getting our bins emptied on time right through to revitalising the town centre which has, in my view, very sadly changed beyond all recognition over the last few years from a vibrant shopping hub to a wasteland of empty units. I believe taxpaying residents deserves more than the Council is currently delivering.

A particular issue I am very troubled by is the rising level of violent crime, anti-social behaviour and drug dealing in the town which has been exacerbated by the closure of our police station that not only provided a safe, open place to residents needing to use it, its presence, without doubt, provided a deterrent against crime in Eastleigh itself and the wider constituency.  We have the right to feel safe in our town and constituency with a visible police presence to provide that comfort and assurance, not a remote office on the outskirts of town that you cannot walk into without making an appointment.

About Me

A bit more about me, I worked for the NHS at Omega House for almost five years, supporting a Clinical Commissioning Group Board which gave me a working insight into how public services are organised and delivered… there is a lot of work to do.  I also worked for the Commissioning Support Unit at Omega House in the Freedom of Info

Since 2023, I've lived in Eastleigh and fallen in love with our community. But there is work to do. My background in e-commerce and the charity sector has taught me that we don't have to abandon our ideals to help people in order to find solutions that actually work.

For most of us, life has never been harder. We've started accepting the bare minimum as a pleasant surprise. I want to hold the council to account, injecting new energy and ideas. Forget party politics; we need realistic, efficient results that make life better for everyone and protect our planet.

Fair Oak and Horton Heath’s longest serving councillor, Nick was Mayor of the Borough in 2023/2024. He is also Vice Chair of the Fair Oak and Horton Heath Parish Council. He led the team that rescued Fair Oak Library from County Council cuts and is an adviser at Citizens Advice. Nick enthusiastically supports the Borough Council’s One Horton Heath project which is on course to provide 2500 much-needed houses at no cost to council taxpayers. He also praises the development of Stoke Common Park. As a Saints season ticket holder, he has recently started to believe in miracles.

My name is Ben Burcombe-Filer, and I am the Conservative candidate for Southampton Test in this general election. 

My journey to this point has always had Southampton Test at its heart. I first came to Southampton Test aged 18 months having been diagnosed with cerebral palsy and written off. My fightback began with the skill help and support of Southampton General, who have been there every step of the way since. 

“My whole life, professional and personal, has been about overcoming challenges, in environments where failure simply is not an option. I hope to be able to use those attributes in serving and representing the residents of Southampton Test. Together we can ensure bold action provides a Secure future for the City.”

My main aims if elected as MP would be

1.	To ensure the people of Southampton Test feel that their voice is heard and they are represented. With a Labour MP since 1997 and a council on which 70% of the seats are held by Labour there is no check or debate currently. The people deserve an MP who will listen, not just hear, and act accordingly. 

2.	Focus on the things that really matter to people. Continuing to cut crime and antisocial behaviour, cleaning up our streets, further elevating the pride Southampton communities feel in their city. 

3.	Ensure the resilience of infrastructure and industry in the city, making sure the port has the capacity to continue to provide quality jobs for residents for years to come by helping to  attract further investment. As well as making sure our infrastructure and transport network is future proofed so the people and business’ of Southampton Test can plan with confidence and thrive.  

I feel strongly that I have a debt to the people and place of Southampton Test, without them I simply would not be here. Now I want to use my skills and experiences to give back.

I’m a local business owner, but more importantly, I’m your neighbour. Like many of you, I care deeply about what happens in our community, and I don’t think our area is getting the focus or representation it deserves.

For years, Eastleigh Borough Council has been dominated by one party with very little meaningful opposition. That lack of balance isn’t healthy for anyone.

As a business owner, I’m particularly concerned about the level of council debt – among the highest in the country – while residents continue to face rising council tax. Too often, large sums of public money are spent without enough scrutiny or challenge.

If you’re looking for someone who will challenge the current leadership, question how your money is spent and stand up for our community, I’d be grateful for your support.

𝗥𝗶𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗱 𝗦𝗵𝘂𝗸𝗲𝗿: 𝗔𝗻 𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗽𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗩𝗼𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗙𝗮𝗶𝗿 𝗢𝗮𝗸 & 𝗛𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗼𝗻 𝗛𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗵
On Thursday, May 7th, use your vote to choose a local champion, not a party politician.
𝗔𝗯𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝗠𝗲:
I am a proud Fair Oak resident, a husband, and a father of two children attending our local state schools. My commitment to our community is personal — I want to ensure this remains a safe, thriving place for all our families to grow up in.
I am deeply involved in our local area, currently serving as Chair of Governors for a nearby Primary School. I am also an active campaigner, serving as a committee member for Action Against Destructive Development, where I have helped organise opposition to inappropriate planning applications. I have spoken directly at planning committees, successfully ensuring that harmful developments were rejected to protect our communities.
My belief in our area extends beyond just planning. I have decided to stand as an Independent candidate because I'm concerned that for too long Liberal Democrat Controlled Eastleigh Borough Council has been making risky financial decisions that threaten our future.
EBC has amassed a staggering total debt of over £580 million. Their latest statement of accounts admits they will need to borrow more to remain a going concern. Source: EBC Statement of Accounts: https://www.eastleigh.gov.uk/.../2024-25-statement-of...
One Horton Heath has been mired in controversy & is a significant contributor to increased debt at EBC. In my opinion OHH represents a considerable overstep for a local authority, both in terms of remit and risk.
While the council focuses on becoming a property developer, the basics are being ignored. Residents in Fair Oak and Horton Heath are facing:
• Chronic traffic congestion that is only getting worse.
• A lack of GP appointments and strained local services.
• Overdevelopment without the necessary infrastructure to support it.
𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗼𝗹𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻: 𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗽𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗔𝗰𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆
This election is not about Westminster or national party politics.

My name is Ben Parry and I have lived with my family in Fair Oak for over 13 years. I believe it’s time you had a credible candidate that understands we can no longer afford to take a ‘one or the other’ approach on the economy and the environment; I firmly believe I am that credible candidate. The Covid pandemic, and the climate crisis, have shown us that governance at any level cannot continue to be ‘business as usual’. It has also emphasised the importance of a strong community. My children attend a junior and secondary school in the local area. Until recently, I was a teacher for 17 years; teaching in local secondary schools and in an Eastleigh sixth form college. Now, as a director of an Eastleigh-based traffic survey company, I see how heavy investment in local housing has not been matched by investment in proper infrastructure. My election would see me focus on:

Standing up for the economy • support local businesses to be successful and sustainable.

Standing up for the community • Help the community transition towards a carbon zero future. • Fight for a green transport infrastructure that alleviates the stress on our roads. 

Standing up for our green spaces • Protect the communal green spaces and ancient woodland that help protect our physical and mental health.

 Most importantly, I would be an independent voice on Eastleigh Borough Council, able to speak up with your best interests at heart, not the best interests of a party.

Prad was one of the leading voices at the Planning Appeal against the County Council’s backing of Cemex’s plan for gravel extraction at Hamble Airfield and 15 years of additional heavy traffic on Hamble Lane. He is continuing his opposition with the support of the community campaign group, Hamble Parish Council and the area’s County Councillor, Cllr Keith House. Prad is backing concerns in Netley from residents worried by speeding traffic through the village and helped secure a reduced speed limit at Grange Road near the Primary School entrance. He is Cabinet Member for Children’s Services at the Borough Council.

Leigh lives in Hedge End with his family and was first elected to the Borough Council four years ago. He also serves as a member and Vice-Chair of Hedge End Town Council. During his time in office, Leigh has supported improvements to local play facilities, including at Nelsons Gardens and Locke Road, as well as proposals for future cycle links connecting Hedge End with Eastleigh and Deer Park School. Professionally, Leigh is the Buying Director for a successful Southampton-based health and beauty company. In his spare time, he enjoys mountain biking and is a keen supporter of Southampton FC.

It's time to stand for the community make change fix issues in the area, and be listened to not just brushed off like we have been for to long stang for change stand for reform

See it, report it, fix it.

Local theatre groups have suffered under the lib dems having prices on charity's go up to cover there costs enough is enough let's make change let's reform for the community

I have lived in Hedge End for 30 years and my children have all made their way through Shamblehurst and Wildern. I am passionate about the area and determined to improve it from within. In particular I believe there has been too much building without suitable infrastructure, and there is far too much traffic on the road, which is bad for the environment and also makes it dangerous for cyclists, particularly school children. I will engage for better bus services and cycling infrastructure, as a start, to alleviate this, whilst showing remarkable energy towards any other case work that arises.

John is a past Chair of Hedge End Town Council and was first elected to the Borough Council last year, following the passing of long-standing councillor Jane Welsh. John has brought up his family in Hedge End and has lived in the area for over 25 years. John was previously a parent Governor at Freegrounds Infants School. Both John and his wife work in the health sector with the NHS, John as a senior manager and his wife as an Advanced Clinical Practitioner nurse. Locally, John has a keen interest in youth provision and employment opportunities for young people.

I am standing as the Green Party candidate for Hedge End South, a community I have always called home and where I am now raising my children. I understand the issues local families face and care deeply about our shared future. I am passionate about improving residents' health and wellbeing, from cleaner air and greener spaces to supporting active lifestyles and strong community networks. I will stand up for sustainable development, better local services, and a fairer future for everyone. I want to ensure Hedge End remains a healthy, thriving place to live for current and future generations.

Elected to the Borough Council for Hiltingbury in 2022, Maud is the Borough Council’s Cabinet Member for Adult Services, helping the Council prepare for local government reorganisation in 2028 when the new Council for South West Hampshire will replace the County and Borough Councils. Maud’s responsibilities include grants to the voluntary sector and support for homelessness and housing.  Having worked as a special needs tutor, this frontline experience has given her a clear understanding of the challenges faced not only by those with additional needs, but also by their families and the professionals who support them.

Shelagh has lived in Chandler’s Ford for her entire life and is proud to be a resident of a thriving community.

Shelagh says “Community is very important to me as I deeply believe that by supporting each other we become stronger. I have a proven record of working with local community groups and teach performing arts in a local school. If elected I will work for our local community to help address the issues residents feel are important to them”.

I’ve lived in Chandler’s Ford for over 20 years, returning to the same road where I grew up and went to school. Having also worked in Surrey and London, I bring both local knowledge and wider experience. I’m standing for Reform UK because I care about our community, supporting the vulnerable, and encouraging personal responsibility. As I approach retirement, I have the time and experience to give back and make a positive difference.

I've lived in Hiltingbury for 10 years, and my family has lived here for over 50 years. I want to make sure Hiltingbury has good public transport links, ensure our local environment is protected and that it is an affordable place to live, like it has been for my family through the years. The Green Party has shown that politics can be done differently, and with an additional seat on the council, we can work towards replacing the "same old same old" way of running things.

The son of a Royal Marine, Bruce was widely travelled in his formative years.  As a consequence, he studied at ten schools before settling down to study at Eastney Modern for Boys, Portsmouth.

Bruce started his career at Portsmouth Dockyard as an apprentice Laboratory Mechanic. Four years later after leaving school without a single O level he had gained sufficient qualifications to go on to study at honours level an Electrical & Electronic Engineering Sandwich degree course at Portsmouth Polytechnic and from there worked in large scale manufacturing around the country, where his highlight was working in an aerospace & automotive factory in Paris with 1,200 French colleagues. Bruce's role at this facility was technical lead for the UK sales office for which learning to speak French was essential.

Years later his manufacturing career effectively came to an end when British American Tobacco closed its manufacturing facility in Southampton due to a change in government legislation. However by then Bruce had gained a merit pass in a Master of Business Administration postgraduate degree at the University of Southampton.

With his manufacturing career firmly behind him, Bruce turned his attention to politics, where in 2006 he became a Councillor at his first attempt in Eastleigh. By 2011, Bruce had gained another postgraduate degree, this time a merit pass in a Master of Public Administration at the University of Portsmouth and serves on four local councils including Hampshire County Council.

His most recent highlight was being invited by the Chinese government to give lectures on British democracy at several universities in southern China. He strongly supports education and manufacturing, and plans to use his influence to encourage Chinese students to study at British universities. To this end Bruce has enrolled on a Mandarin course at Southampton University.

With over 30 years’ experience in enforcement, crime reduction, and community safety, I have seen first-hand how poor political guidance within local government can undermine effective public services. Having lived in Southampton all my life, and as an Eastleigh resident for more than 28 years, I bring deep local knowledge and a long‑standing commitment to the communities I hope to serve.

Throughout my career, I have built and led high‑performing teams across local government and policing, including Hampshire’s top‑performing environmental enforcement team. I have a strong record of modernising processes, strengthening multi‑agency partnerships, and delivering initiatives that improve public confidence and reduce crime.

I am a local resident of West End and a mum of two, with a strong interest in community wellbeing and support for families. Through my own experiences, I understand how important local services, healthcare and inclusive support systems are to everyday lives.

I'm standing as a Green Party candidate to represent a perspective rooted in real life and lived experience. I care about creating a community that is supportive, accessible and fair for everyone. 
While I'm new to this, I'm committed to learning, listening, and being part of a positive and constructive voice locally.

David lives in Chartwell Green and is Vice Chair of West End Parish Council. He has a long history of community involvement, having chaired West End Preschool and having served as a school governor as well as on the Parish Council. David has taken a keen interest in protecting green spaces such as West End Copse and Telegraph Woods and has been concerned with proposals from the Utilita Bowl for additional housing on the site. He believes in practical, pragmatic solutions that protect green spaces and local services, while ensuring residents are not digitally excluded.

🙋‍♂️ Introducing Myself — Steve Humby, Reform UK Candidate for West End South

Hi everyone! I'm Steve Humby, and I'm standing as your Reform UK candidate for West End South on Eastleigh Borough Council and Hampshire County Council at the May 7th local elections.

I live in Hamble and have proudly called this corner of Hampshire home for 38 years. You might know me from my time serving the community — I spent 31 years as a Retained Firefighter and 8 years as a Wholetime Firefighter with Hampshire & IOW Fire and Rescue Service. I also served as a Special Constable based at Bitterne and Park Gate, and as an Ambulance Co-Responder. Public service has always been at the heart of who I am.

So what's brought me to politics? Honestly — frustration. A recent experience dealing with my Parish Council opened my eyes to just how painful and unnecessarily bureaucratic local government can be. If it felt that way to me, I can only imagine how it feels for other ordinary residents trying to get things done. That has to change.

If elected, my priorities will be:
✅ Tackling Eastleigh's growing council debt
✅ Helping ease the cost of living for local families
✅ Simply working for the people of West End — not for the system

I'm not a career politician. I'm a retired firefighter who has spent nearly four decades living and serving in this community. I'd love your support — and more than that, I'd love to hear what matters most to you.

👍 Follow my Facebook page to keep up with my campaign, and feel free to drop me a message anytime. Let's work together for West End — vote Steve Humby on May 7th!

#ReformUK #WestEndSouth #Eastleigh #Hampshire #StepUpForWestEnd #VoteSteveHumby

Dave has lived in West End/Eastleigh Borough for over 20 years. He is a hardworking, community-minded candidate who has worked for the NHS since 2002 as a senior software engineer for University Hospital Southampton. He has served on the board of a number of local charities and chaired Eastleigh Green Party. Dave is the proud father of two (almost) grown-up boys and is passionate about building a sustainable, inclusive, and hope-filled society for them and future generations. He is also a musician, a YouTube content creator and has a keen interest in local history and heritage.
